ROGII Glossary — visually explained

Every term you need to read the ROGII Wellbore Geology Prediction problem, with diagrams. For the formal problem statement see competition_overview.html; for the data EDA see eda_report.html.

1. The whole task in one picture

A vertical typewell has already been drilled through the geological column; it gives us a GR ↔ TVT dictionary. A horizontal well is now being drilled sideways through the same formation; we measure its GR along the way. The task: from the horizontal well's GR observations + the typewell dictionary + the wellbore trajectory, recover where the bit sits in the geological column at every measured-depth point. That “where” is the TVT coordinate.

2. Three depth coordinates: MD, TVD, TVT

TermWhat it measuresWhere in the dataRole
MD Distance along the wellbore from surface (arc length). Always increases as we drill further. Horizontal CSV column MD. Step is exactly 1 ft per row in every well. input feature
Z / TVD True vertical depth from surface, in geometric (Cartesian) coordinates. Horizontal CSV column Z — signed elevation (negative = deeper). input feature
TVT True Vertical Thickness — position in the geological column measured against the typewell's vertical reference. This is what we predict. Horizontal CSV column TVT (full in training, hidden post-PS in test). TVT_input is the masked version available at inference. prediction target
TVT ≠ TVD. Two wells in different parts of the field may have the same TVT (same geological layer) but very different TVD (different surface elevations, different dipping geometry). TVT lives in the typewell's frame — it is designed to be a coordinate that moves with the geology, not with the geometric depth.

2.1  TVT in detail — the same scale, two ways to obtain it

This is the single most important concept in the dataset: both wells share the SAME TVT scale (purple axis on the left of panels a and b) — it is the “floor numbering” of the geological building. The only difference is how we get TVT for each well type.

Well typeHow TVT is obtainedWhat it looks like in the CSV
Typewell (vertical) Direct measurement. The well drills straight down through every layer; TVT = its physical depth (panel a). Like an elevator that records its floor as it descends. TVT column, sampled every 0.5 ft of true depth. Range ~11,224 – 11,871 ft for the demo well.
Horizontal well (lateral) Must be inferred. The bit travels sideways through dipping layers (panel b). At each MD point we ask: “which layer is the bit in?” That layer's TVT in the typewell is the bit's TVT — but to identify the layer we need to match GR signatures. TVT column (training only); TVT_input available only before PS at inference. This is the column we predict.

Panel (c) shows real data from the demo well: TVT (purple) lives in the geological coordinate frame and stays within a relatively narrow band (the Eagle Ford zone, ~11,200–11,800 ft); Z (blue) is the geometric elevation in the surveying coordinate frame and tracks the wellbore's actual physical position. They are clearly two different coordinates of the same MD axis. The competition asks us to recover the purple curve given the geometric Z, the observed GR, and the typewell GR(TVT) dictionary.

3. GR — the gamma-ray log, geology's fingerprint

GR is the natural gamma radiation emitted by the rock the sensor passes through. Different lithologies have different characteristic GR levels:

  • Shales — high GR (organic-rich; contain potassium, thorium, uranium). Eagle Ford in this dataset is a shale → high GR.
  • Limestones / chalks / sandstones — low GR.

Because every formation has its own GR signature, the GR sequence with depth is a unique fingerprint of the local stratigraphy. That fingerprint is what we match between horizontal and vertical wells.

4. Typewell vs horizontal well

TermWhat it isIts role here
Typewell A nearby vertical well drilled through the full geological column. Sampled every 0.5 ft of TVT. reference / dictionary — provides GR(TVT) so we can look up the TVT of a given GR signature.
Horizontal well The lateral well we are predicting. Sampled every 1 ft of MD. the well we predict on — gives the live GR observation + trajectory.

The two GR plots look very different on purpose: the typewell's y-axis is TVT (depth, inverted), the horizontal well's x-axis is MD (along-hole distance). The competition task is to figure out, for every point on the right, which point on the left it corresponds to.

5. Horizons / formation tops

A horizon is a geological boundary — the surface that separates two formations. In this dataset, the six formations from shallow to deep are:

  1. ANCC — Anacacho (limestone, top of the column)
  2. ASTNU — Austin Chalk (Upper)
  3. ASTNL — Austin Chalk (Lower)
  4. EGFDU — Eagle Ford (Upper) — the productive shale zone
  5. EGFDL — Eagle Ford (Lower) — still the productive zone
  6. BUDA — Buda Limestone (base of interest)

In the training horizontal CSVs, each row contains six extra columns (one per formation) giving the absolute elevation of each formation top at the trajectory point. These describe the geological structure under the bit and are removed in the test set — they would leak the answer.

Use the formation-top columns as auxiliary supervision. They are train-only, so they can't be inference inputs — but an ML model trained to predict TVT and the six formation-top elevations simultaneously learns better representations than one trained on TVT alone.

6. Prediction Start (PS)

PS is the MD index where the known TVT_input stops and the prediction zone begins. In the master diagram above, the dashed red line marks PS. Before PS, the model can see the true TVT (via TVT_input); after PS, it must predict.

Median in this dataset: PS sits around 25% of the way through the well, so the prediction zone is about 75% of the lateral. See eda_report.html §4 for the exact distribution.

7. The whole thing as an inverse problem

Given an observed GR sequence along the horizontal well and a typewell GR-vs-TVT dictionary, we want to recover the TVT(MD) function. This is literally inversion: solve for the unknown geological position given forward-model observations. Concrete methods (DTW alignment, Tikhonov-regularized Gauss-Newton, sequential Kalman/particle filters, MDN multi-modal inversion) are graded in competition_overview.html §8.

8. Cheat-sheet table — every term's role

TermSymbol / columnRoleAvailable at inference?
Measured DepthMD input (sequence index) Yes
Trajectory coordinatesX, Y, Z input (geometry) Yes
Gamma RayGR input (the observation) Yes (~28% missing on average)
Known TVT before PSTVT_input input (warm start) Yes (before PS only)
True Vertical ThicknessTVT target No (this is what we predict)
Typewell GRtypewell GR reference (vertical dictionary) Yes
Typewell TVTtypewell TVT reference (depth axis for the dictionary) Yes
Typewell geology labeltypewell Geology reference (sparse formation labels) Yes
Formation tops (6)ANCC, ASTNU, ASTNL, EGFDU, EGFDL, BUDA auxiliary training supervision No — train only
